full_response:
Step 1:  Define the opening angle  
Let the apex of the cone be at the origin and let the symmetry axis point in the +z–direction.  
The opening (half-)angle α is the angle between a generator of the cone and the +z axis.  
For a finite cone of height h and base‐radius r

tan α = r / h.           (1)

Step 2:  Equation of the conical surface in Cartesian coordinates  
For any point (x,y,z) on the surface the distance ρ⊥ from the axis equals

ρ⊥ = √(x² + y²).

From simple trigonometry ρ⊥ = z tan α, so the surface is

√(x² + y²) = z tan α  or  x² + y² = z² tan² α.    (2)

Step 3:  Equation in spherical coordinates  
In spherical coordinates (ρ,θ,φ) the polar angle θ measured from the +z-axis is constant and equals α:

θ = α, 0 ≤ φ < 2π, ρ ≥ 0.                          (3)

Step 4:  Finite vs. infinite cone  
•  Finite cone: 0 ≤ z ≤ h (or 0 ≤ ρ ≤ h /cos α) together with (2) or (3).  
•  If we drop the upper bound on z (or ρ) the same equations describe an
   infinitely long cone extending upward from the apex.

Step 5:  Allowed range of the opening angle  
For a genuine cone we require the side to make a non-zero acute angle with the axis:

0 < α < π/2   (0° < α < 90°).

At α = 0 the cone collapses into the axis, and at α = π/2 it flattens into a plane.

\boxed{\text{Surface equation: } x^{2}+y^{2}=z^{2}\tan ^{2}\alpha \quad(\text{with } z\ge 0)}
\\
\boxed{\text{or equivalently } \theta=\alpha \text{ in spherical coordinates}}
\\
\boxed{\text{Valid for } 0<\alpha<\dfrac{\pi}{2};\ \text{dropping an upper bound on }z\text{ gives the infinite cone}}
extracted_answer: \text{Valid for } 0<\alpha<\dfrac{\pi}{2};\ \text{dropping an upper bound on }z\text{ gives the infinite cone}
